Facts.text: Approximate domestication of the horse in the Eurasian steppes near Dereivka, central Ukraine (hypothesis only)
Hugh Capet (Hugh the Great) crowned King of the Franks in Noyon
King Philip Augustus of France conquers Rouen

Albert I, son of Rudolf I of Hapsburg made sole ruler of the Duchies of Austria and Styria under Treaty of Rheinfelden, removing his brother Rudolph II as co-ruler (leads later to Rudolph's son murdering his uncle)
Pope Pius II opens the Council of Mantua to call for a new crusade against the Ottoman Empire  - crusade never amounts to much (Council disbanded January 1560)
Matthias of Hungary takes Vienna from Holy Roman Emperor Frederick III
First written record of Scotch whisky appears in the Exchequer Rolls of Scotland, Friar John Cor is listed as the distiller
Duke of Alva oversees beheading of 18 nobles in Brussels as part of Council of Troubles/Council of Blood
Valse Dimitri forms his 2nd Russian anti-government
1st earthquake recorded in US, at Plymouth, Massachusetts
France and Portugal sign anti-Spanish covenant
Russian Tsar Alexis throws English merchants out of Moscow
1st Quakers arrives in New Amsterdam (NY)
Mary Dyer is hanged for defying a law banning Quakers from the Massachusetts Bay Colony
Battle of Öland: naval battle between Danish-Dutch fleet and the Swedish navy in the Baltic Sea
The Scottish Covenanters defeat John Graham of Claverhouse at the Battle of Drumclog
French troops conquer Antwerp
1st US congressional act becomes law (on administering oaths)
Kentucky admitted as 15th US state

Glorious First of June; first naval battle between Britain (under Admiral Lord Howe) and France (Vice-Admiral Louis Thomas Villaret de Joyeuse) during French Revolutionary Wars. Britain gains tactical win.
Last of Britain's troops withdraws from USA
Tennessee admitted as 16th US state
Allardyce Barclay begins a bet of walking 1 mile every hour for 1,000 hours. Each hour he walked a mile round trip from his home
Captain John Lawrence utters Navy motto "Don't give up the ship"
British explorer James Clark Ross discovers the magnetic North Pole on the west coast of Boothia peninsula
HMS Beagle for anchor in Port Famine, Magallanes Street
6th national black convention (Philadelphia)
It snows in Buffalo and Rochester, NY, and Cleveland, Ohio
Homing pigeon completes 11,000 km trip (Namibia-London) in 55 days
US adventurer William Walker conquers Nicaragua, reestablishes slavery
Charles Baudelaire's Les Fleurs du Mal (The Flowers of Evil) is published.
1st skirmish in US Civil War at Fairfax Court House, Virginia
British territorial waters & ports off-limits during Civil War
Skirmish at Arlington Mills, Virginia
US & Confederacy simultaneously stop mail interchange
2nd/last day of battle at Fair Oaks/7 Pines Virginia (11,165 casualties)
African Slave Trade Treaty Act: Bilateral treaty between the US and UK abolishing the slave trade in all US possessions
-Nov] Shenandoah Valley campaign
Battle of Cold Harbor, Virginia (Gaines' Mill, Gaines' Farm)
Confederate cruiser Georgia sold to a British merchant in Liverpool
General Dutch Typographer Union forms
Renegade Irish Fenians from US invade Fort Erie, Ontario
Texas constitutional convention meets in Austin
Treaty of Bosque Redondo is signed allowing the Navajos to return to their lands in Arizona and New Mexico
Charles Albanel's expedition sets off for Hudson Bay overland accompanied by sixteen Amerindian canoeists (they will be the first Europeans to reach it overland about 25 days later)
Society of American Artists forms
US troops authorized to pursue bandits into Mexico
The first pay telephone service in the United States is installed in New Haven, Connecticut
US census is 50,155,783
Bell Phone opens 1st Dutch telephone exchange
Ridden by outstanding English jockey Fred Archer, Iroquois wins the Epsom Derby to become the first American-owned and bred horse to win a European classic race
The railroads of the Southern United States convert 11,000 miles of track from a five foot rail gauge to standard gauge, beginning May 31
California gets its 1st seismograph
US census at 62,622,250
Opera "Falstaff" is produced (Berlin)
Hotelier Caesar Ritz opens the Ritz Hotel in Paris, the first hotel with electricity on every floor and separate bathrooms
Trans-Mississippi International Exposition opens in Omaha
British army occupies Pretoria, South Africa
-27°F (-33°C), Sarmiento, Argentina (South American record)
John Krohn begins walk around perimeter of US, which took 357 days
Alaska-Yukon-Pacific Exposition opens in Seattle
Sportclub Enschede soccer club forms in Enschede, Netherlands; merge with Enschedese Boys to form FC Twente in 1965
1st Inter-Empire Sports Championships close in London
1st US group insurance policy written in Passaic, New Jersey
Dutch soccer club Stormvogels forms in Ijmuiden; merges with VSV to form Telstar in 1963
The Serbian government concludes a ten-year treaty with Greece against Bulgaria; Serbia wishes to pursue Macedonian aspirations with Greece's help
US Senate confirms Louis Brandeis for the Supreme Court of the United States, by a vote of 47 to 22, over 4 months after his nomination
Boston Braves first baseman Hank Gowdy is first active MLB player to enlist for service in World War I; only player to fight in both World War I & II
Chicago White Sox losing 5-4 against NY Yankees, load the bases in 9th with no outs; Chick Gandil lines to Frank Baker who turns a rare game winning triple play
Rhineland Republic forms in Wiesbaden
Adolfo de la Huerta becomes President of Mexico
Dutch soccer club RKSV forms in Volendam; merges with FC Volendam in 1977
Royal Ulster Constabulary is founded
New York Giants rout Philadelphia Phillies, 22-5 at the Baker Bowl; first time in 20th century a MLB team has scored in every inning
Ignacy Mocicki elected President of Poland
Peace Bridge between US and Canada opens
Century of Progress world's fair opens in Chicago
"Lux Radio Theater" moved from NYC to Hollywood
Queen Mary completes its maiden voyage, arriving in NY
Chicago White Sox pitcher Bill Dietrich no-hits St Louis Browns, 8-0 at Comiskey Park, Chicago
Prince Konoye becomes Japanese premier
British submarine "Thetis" sinks in Liverpool Bay with all 99 aboard
First night MLB game takes place at Philadelphia's Shribe Park; Phillies lose 5-2 to Pittsburgh Pirates
Retired German Colonel-general Gerd von Runstedt returns to service
Coffee & tea rationed in Holland
Major GeneralBernard Montgomeryreturns to London
Nazi occupiers kick Jews out of Dutch air guard
12.59" (31.98 cm) rainfall, in Burlington, Kansas (state 24-hr record)
British troops occupy Baghdad, Iraq
Germany bans all Catholic publications
Germany occupies Crete after Allied evacuation
Germany shoots down a civilian flight from Lisbon to London, 13 passengers, included actor Leslie Howard, and 4 crew die
Pittsburgh Pirates pitcher Rip Sewell patents his "eephus" or "blooper ball" pitch; uses pitch on way to a 20-win campaign
Nazi occupiers make it punishable to give aid to allied pilots
Washington Senators MLB outfielder Stan Spence goes 6-for-6 in an 11-5 win over the St. Louis Browns; collects 5 singles and a home run
WLB-AM in Minneapolis Minn changes call letters to KUOM
OPA, which issued WW II rationing coupons, disbands
The development of photosensitive glass, which had occurred ten years previously, is announced publicly
Israel & Arabs agree to a cease fire
British government grants Cyrenaica (East-Libya) independence
KSL TV channel 5 in Salt Lake City, UT (CBS) begins broadcasting
WKZO (now WWMT) TV channel 3 in Kalamazoo, MI (CBS) 1st broadcast
1st self-contained titanium plant opens (Henderson Nevada)
International Cheese treaty signed
Massey Commission issues its landmark report in Canada, advocating for funding wide range of cultural activities (resulted in  the founding of the National Library of Canada)
KMJ (now KSEE) TV channel 24 in Fresno, CA (NBC) begins broadcasting
WDAY TV channel 6 in Fargo, ND (ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
Czech distance runner Emile Zatopek breaks his own 10,000m world record, clocking 28:54.2 in Brussels, Belgium
Tunisian nationalist leader Habib Bourguiba returns to Tunisia after imprisonment and exile in France
Don Bowden becomes first American to run a sub-4 minute mile (3:58.7) at the Pacific Association AAU Meet in Stockton, California
Graham Greene's stage drama "The Potting Shed" closes at the John Golden Theatre, NYC, after 143 performances
Belgian christian-democrats win parliamentary election
The Riverside International Raceway in Southern California opens with three 500-mile races in one weekend; Eddie Gray wins the Crown America 500 for NASCAR Grand National cars
Constitution of Tunisia promulgated (National Day)
Two-time defending champion Monterrey, Mexico ruled ineligible to compete in Little League Baseball World Series for using players outside predetermined geographical area
WDTV TV channel 5 in Clarksburg-Weston, West Vrirginia (CBS) begins broadcasting
FM multiplex stereo broadcasting 1st heard
Oscar 2 (ham radio satellite) launched into Earth orbit
USAF Maj Robert M White takes X-15 to 40,420 m
"El Watusi" by Ray Barreto hits #17
Rolling Stones arrive in New York's Kennedy International Airport for 1st US tour, greeted by about 500 fans
Arno Penzias and Robert Woodrow Wilson at Bell Labs detect 3°K primordial background radiation - helping confirm The Big Bang theory (1978 Nobel Prize)
Coal mine explosion in Fukuoka Japan kills 236
Robert Manry begins his 78 day voyage to sail a 13.5-foot yacht "Tinkerbelle" across the Atlantic Ocean
2,400 people attend White House Conference on Civil Rights
Joaquin Balaguer elected president of Dominican Republic for the second time
Shortwave station Radio NY Worldwide changes calls from WRUL to WNYW
Mayor-council form of government instituted for Washington, D.C.
Simon & Garfunkel's single "Mrs. Robinson" from "The Graduate" hits #1 (first rock song to win a Grammy for Record of the Year)
St. Louis Cardinals side-arming left-hander Joe Hoerner ties NL record for most consecutive strikeouts by a relief pitcher, fanning the final 6 batters to earn a 6-5 victory over the New York Mets
Tobacco advertising is banned on Canadian radio & TV
Soyuz 9 launched into Earth orbit for 18 days
Iraq nationalizes Iraq Petroleum Company's (IPC) concession owned by British Petroleum, Royal Dutch-Shell, Compagnie Francaise des Petroles, Mobil and Standard Oil of New Jersey
Tswanaland becomes Bophuthatswana in South Africa
Eight OPEC countries raise price of petroleum by 11.9 percent
George Harrison's "Living in the Material World" album goes gold, 2 days after its US release
Greek President Papadopoulos asks for "parliamentary presidential republic"
"My Girl Bill" by Jim Stafford hits #12
Arab oil ministers decide to end most restrictions on exports of oil to the United States but continue embargo against the Netherlands, Portugal, South Africa, and Rhodesia
Bundy victim Brenda Ball disappears from Burien, Washington
Chemical plant explodes in Flixborough Lincolnshire killing 28 in UK
The Heimlich maneuver for rescuing choking victims is published in the journal Emergency Medicine
Cars in the Netherlands required to have seatbelts
Ronnie Wood replaces Mick Taylor as Rolling Stones guitarist, who was asked to leave
Great Britain and Iceland end the "cod war"
British Virgin Islands adopts constitution
Dutch soccer club FC Volendam is established as a result of split up with RKSV; 6-time Eerste Divisie champions
Russia charges Jewish rights activist Anatoly Shcharansky with treason
England cricket fast bowler Chris Old becomes only third man to capture 4 wickets in 5 balls in a Test, as Pakistan dismissed for 164 in 1st Test at Edgbaston; Old 5 for 70
The first international applications under the Patent Cooperation Treaty are filed
Bishop Abel Muzorewa is sworn in as Zimbabwe Rhodesia's 1st black Prime Minister, the first leader of a black-led government in 90 years
Los Angeles passes its first homosexual rights bill
Ted Coombs begins 5,193 mile roller skate from Los Angeles to New York City
Vizianagaram district is formed in Andhra Pradesh, India.
Wings release "Old Siam, Sir"
ANC sets fire to Sasol oil installations in South Africa
First transmission of the Cable News Network or CNN
Jaffna Public Library, repository of Tamil culture and one of the largest libraries in Asia, is burnt to the ground by a Sinhalese crowd
Charles the Great (akaCharlemagne) opens general synod in Frankfurt
Peking [Beijing], then a city of over one million, under the control of Jurchen ruler Emperor Xuanzong of Jin, is captured and looted for a month by the Mongols underGenghis Khan
Anne Boleyncrowned Queen of England
Holy Roman Emperor Ferdinand I and Ottoman SultanSuleiman the Magnificentsign treaty
English KingCharles II& French KingLouis XIVsign secret anti-Dutch treaty
Napoleon Bonaparteswears fidelity to the Constitution of France
Charles Darwinreturns to Cape Town in South Africa
Sojourner Truthhears the Spirit of God and changes her name from Isabella Baumfree, leaving NY to begin travelling giving speeches against slavery
Revolutionary newspaper "Neue Rheinische Zeitung" founded byKarl Marx,Friedrich Engelsand the Communist League in Cologne
GeneralRobert E. Leeassumes command after Joe Johnston is injured at Seven Pines
Thomas Edisongranted his first patent for the Electric Vote Recorder (U.S. Patent 90,646)
Lewis and ClarkCentennial Exposition opens in Portland, Oregon
Canadian aceBilly Bishopdowns 6 aircraft over a three-day span, including German ace Paul Bilik, reclaiming his top scoring title from James McCudden
Over 50,000 Fascists gather for a meeting in Bologna whereMussoliniwarns that he will lead a full-scale revolt against a government favoring 'anti-Fascist reaction'
Future NY Yankees Hall of FamerLou Gehrigpinch hits for shortstop Pee-Wee Wanninge in a 5-3 loss to Washington; first of record 2,130 consecutive games
Franz von Papenbecomes Reich Chancellor of Germany
Edith Whartonsuffers a heart attack and collapses at the French country home of Ogden Codman
First major boxing match on US television is the heavyweight bout from NY's Yankee Stadium between former world championMax Baerand Lou Nova; Nova wins by TKO in 11th round
Future Hall of Fame outfielderMel Otthits his 400th career HR and his 1,500th RBI to help New York Giants to a 3-2 win over the Cincinnati Reds
Catholic church puts Andre Gides "Labor" on the index
Charles de Gaulleelected premier of France
Jomo Kenyattais sworn in as the first prime minister of the Dominion of Kenya
George Harrisonis impressed by sitaristRavi Shankar's concert in London
"Everything Is Beautiful" byRay Stevenshits #1
MLB CommissionerBowie Kuhnreprimands Houston Astros pitcherJim Boutonfor writing memoir "Ball Four"
Dmitri Shostakovich's 15th Symphony, Dutch premieres in West Berlin
West German police arrest Red Army Faction leaderAndreas Baader
Paul McCartney& Wings release single "Live & Let Die"
California Angels pitcherNolan Ryanrecords his record equalling 4th MLB career no-hitter in beating the Baltimore Orioles, 1-0
Future England cricket captainDavid Gowermakes his Test debut in 1st Test against Pakistan in Birmingham; England win by an innings and 57 runs
High Council destroys judgment against Dutch war criminalPieter Menten
American authorOctavia E. Butler's breakout novel "Kindred" is published
Barbra Streisandappears at an ACLU Benefit in California
First basemanSteve Garvey, hits the 7,000th Brooklyn / LA Dodgers home run in a 9-5 loss at home to the Atlanta Braves

Flemish physicianAndreas Vesaliuspublishes "De humani corporis fabrica (On the fabric of the human body in seven books)" a major step forward in understanding human anatomy [date is representative as exact date of publication unknown]
Battle of Öland: allied Danish-Dutch forces defeat the Swedish navy in the Baltic Sea, during the Scanian War (1675–79)
Boston Port Act: Following the passage of the act, the British government orders Port of Boston closed to punish colonists for the Boston Tea Party
Napoleon Eugene, the last dynastic Bonaparte, is killed serving with British forces in the Anglo-Zulu War. He is buried in Farnborough, Hampshire.
Compulsory driving tests and license plates introduced in the United Kingdom
Allied generalsBernard Montgomery, George S. Patton, Omar Bradley, Miles Dempsey and Harry Crerar meet in Portsmouth, England just prior to D-Day
SS officerAdolf Eichmannis executed in Israel after being found guilty of war crimes
EMI releases The Beatles' album "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band" nation-wide in the UK; it goes to number one for 22 weeks in the UK
Mstislav I of Kiev, Mstislav the Great, Grand Prince of Kiev (1125-32), born in Turov (d. 1132)
Thomas of Brotherton, 1st Earl of Norfolk, 5th son of Edward I of England, born in Brotherton, England (d. 1338)
Tiedemann Giese, Polish Catholic bishop and theologian, born in Danzig, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1550)[1][2]
Wilhelm von Grumbach, German adventurer (tried to incite German Knights against Holy Roman Empire), born in Rimpar (d. 1567)
Allesandre Spontone, Bolognese composer and Maestro Di Capella, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. c. 1590)
Robert Cecil, 1st Earl of Salisbury and English Prime Minister (1598-1612), born in Westminster, Salisbury (d. 1612)
Felician Schwab, German organist, composer, and Franciscan monk, baptized in Altdorf, Swabia (d. 1661)
Geminiano Montanari, Italian physicist and astronomer known for discovering that Algol is a variable star, born in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1687)[1]
Georg Muffat, baroque composer, born in Megève, Duchy of Savoy (d. 1704)
Francesco Scipione, marchese di Maffei, Italian archaeologist and dramatist (Merope), born in Verona, Republic of Venice (d. 1755)
Johan Runius, Swedish poet (Dudaim), born in Larvs parish, Sweden (d. 1713)
Frederigo Fiorillo, Italian violist, mandolinist, and composer, baptized in Brunswick, Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1823)
Edmund Ignatius Rice, Irish missionary and founder of the Christian Brothers and Presentation Brothers, born in Callan, Ireland (d. 1844)
Christian Gottlob Saupe, composer, born in Wechselburg, Electoral Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1819)
Christiane Vulpius, German lover then wife of Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, born in Weimar (d. 1816)
Friedrich Ludwig Seidel, German composer, born in Treuenbrietzen, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1831)
Józef Elsner, Polish composer, musicologist, and teacher of Chopin, born in Grodków, Poland (d. 1854)
Ferdinando Paer, Italian opera composer (Agnese), and conductor (Paris Opéra-Italien, 1812-23), born in Parma, Duchy of Parma  (d. 1839)
George Schetky, Scottish-American cellist, music teacher, and composer, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1831)
Carl von Clausewitz, Prussian army General (Napoleanic Wars) and military theorist (Vom Kriege (On War)), born in Burg bei Magdeburg, Kingdom of Prussia (now Germany) (d. 1831)
Ferdinand Raimund, Austrian actor and playwright (The Spendthrift, The Maid from Fairyland), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1836)
Salomon Judah Rappoport, Galician-Czech rabbi and scholar (Ereg miliem), born in Lemberg, Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria (d. 1867)
Edward Deas Thomson, British-Australian politician, chancellor of the University of Sydney, and member of the Legislative Council of NSW, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1879)[1]
Mikhail Glinka, Novosspaskoye Russian composer (Kamarinskaya; Ruslan and Ludmilla), born in Novospasskoye, Smolensk, Russian Empire (d. 1857) [birth OS=May 20]
John B Floyd, American Governor (Virginia), Secretary of War (1857-60) and Confederate general, born in Blacksburg, Virginia (d. 1863)
Otto of Greece, King of Greece (1832-62), born in Salzburg, Bavaria (d. 1862)
Carl Bechstein, German piano maker, born in Gotha, Duchy of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha (d. 1900)
Hermann Zopff, German composer, born in Glogau, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1883)
John Bell Hood, American Confederate general during the American Civil War (Army of Tennessee), born in Owingsville, Kentucky (d. 1879)
John Marshall Harlan, American lawyer and Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court (1877-1911), born in Boyle County, Kentucky (d. 1911)
Henry Faulds, British surgeon, fingerprinting pioneer, and founder of the Tokyo Institute for the Blind, born in Beith, Scotland (d. 1930)[1]
Galusha Pennypacker, American union general during the Civil War (potential youngest brigadier general), born in Valley Forge, Pennsylvania (d. 1916) [birth year disputed]
John J. Toffey, American Civil War hero and Medal of Honor recipient, born in Dutchess County, New York (d. 1911)
Otto Valdemar Malling, Danish organist and composer (Det hellige Land, opus 46), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1915)
William Wilfred Campbell, Canadian poet (Beyond the Hills of Dream, Ian of the Orcades), born in Newmarket, Ontario (d. 1918)
António Feijó, Portuguese poet and diplomat, born in Ponte de Lima, Portugal (d. 1917)
Ada Jane Jones, English-American popular singer, among the earliest female singers to be recorded, born in Lancashire, England (d. 1922)
John Masefield, British writer and poet (Salt-Water Ballads), Poet Laureate (1930-67), born in Ledbury, Herefordshire (d. 1967)
Max Emmerich, American athlete and gymnast (Olympic gold, triathlon 1904), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1956)
John Drinkwater, English poet and playwright (Abraham Lincoln), born in Leytonstone, Essex (d. 1937)
Clive Brook, British actor and director (List of Adrian Messenger), born in London, England (d. 1974)
Charles Kay Ogden, British writer, philosopher, and linguist (Basic English, The Cambridge Magazine), born in Fleetwood, England (d. 1957)[1][2]
Frank Morgan [Francis Wuppermann], American silent and sound film character actor (The Wizard of Oz; The Affairs of Cellini), born in New York City (d. 1949)
Amanullah Khan, Emir and King of Afghanistan (1919-28), born in Paghman, Afghanistan (d. 1960)
Samuel L M Barlow II, American composer (Mon Ami Pierrot), born in New York City (d. 1982)
Frank D. Smith, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame administrator (Beaches/Greater Toronto Hockey League), born in Chatham, Ontario (d. 1964)
Dinorá de Carvalho, Brazilian pianist, conductor, music educator, and composer (Missa Profundis), born in Uberaba, Brazil (d. 1980)
Curtis Stevens, American bobsledder (Olympic gold 2-man [with brother Hubert] 1932), born in Lake Placid, New York (d. 1979)
Edgar "Cookie" Fairchild, American bandleader (Jerry Colonna Show), born in New York City (d. 1975)
Lieb Glantz, Ukrainian-born tenor, composer and Zionist, born in Kyiv, Ukraine (d. 1964)
Edward Charles Titchmarsh, English mathematician known for his work in analytic number theory (Introduction to the Theory of Fourier Integrals), born in Newbury, England (d. 1963)[1]
Werner Janssen, American conductor and composer (New Years Eve in NY), born in New York City (d. 1990)
Tom Gorman, Australian rugby league centre and captain (10 Tests; Queensland 34 games), born in Charters Towers, Australia (d. 1978)
Percy Whitlock, English composer and organist, born in Chatham, England (d. 1946)
Vasyl Vsevolod Velychkovsky, Ukrainian Catholic bishop and martyr who was beatified by Pope John Paul II in 2001, born in Stanislaviv, Austria-Hungary (d. 1973)[1]
Robert Newton, British actor (Treasure Island, Henry V, Around the World in 80 Days), born in Shaftesbury, Dorset, England (d. 1956)
Frank Whittle, English RAF engineer air officer and inventor of the turbojet engine, born in Coventry, England (d. 1996)[1]
Percy Edwards, English entertainer (known for his animal impressions), born in Suffolk, England (d. 1996)
Hans Vogt, Norwegian linguist, born in Fredrikstad, Norway (d. 1986)
Szymon Goldberg, Polish-American violinist, and conductor (Netherlands Chamber Orchestra), born in Włocławek, Poland (d. 1993)
Gertrude Michael, American actress (Cleopatra, Menace, Club Havana, caged), born in Talladega, Alabama (d. 1964)
Bill Deedes [William Francis Deedes], British politician, journalist, and editor of The Daily Telegraph (1974-86), born in Hampstead, England (d. 2007)[1]
Bart Howard [Howard Joseph Gustafson], American songwriter ("Fly Me To The Moon"), born in Burlington, Iowa (d. 2004)
John Randolph [Emanuel Hirsch Cohen], American actor (King Kong, Lucan, Lucas Tanner, Angie), born in New York City (d. 2004)
Ki Aldrich, American College Football Hall of Fame linebacker (TCU; Pro Bowl 1939, 42; Chicago Cardinals, Washington Redskins), born in Rogers, Texas (d. 1983)
William Standish Knowles, American chemist and winner of the 2001 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, born in Taunton, Massachusetts (d. 2012)[1][2]

Nelson Riddle, American Grammy Award-winning bandleader, conductor, and orchestrator for Capitol and Reprise Records (Frank Sinatra, Ella Fitzgerald, Judy Garland, Dean Martin, Rosemary Clooney, Linda Ronstadt), born in Oradell, New Jersey (d. 1985)
Joan Caulfield, American model and actress (My Favorite Husband), born in West Orange, New Jersey (d. 1991)
Joan Copeland, American film and TV soap actress ("Roseland"; "Search for Tomorrow"), born in New York City (d. 2022)
Lala Abdul Rashid, Pakistani field hockey goalkeeper (Olympic gold 1960), born in Rawalpindi, Pakistan (d. 1988)
Povel Ramel, Swedish singer, pianist, author, composer, and comedian (Ratataa or Staffan Stolle Story, The Great Amateur), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2007)[1]
Herbie Lovelle, America drummer, studio musician (Hot Lips Page) and actor (Law & Order), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2009)
Paula Hinton, English ballet dancer, born in Ilford, Essex, England (d. 1996)
William Sloane Coffin, American clergyman and civil rights activist, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2006)
Dilia Díaz Cisneros, Venezuelan teacher and poet, born in El Hatillo, Venezuela (d. 2017)
Richard Erdman, American actor (Stalag 17, Anything Goes), born in Enid, Oklahoma (d. 2019)
Andy Griffith, American actor, comedian, television producer (Andy Griffithe Show; Matlock), Southern gospel singer, and writer, born in Mount Airy, North Carolina (d. 2012)
Darel Dieringer, American auto racer (181 Monster Energy NASCAR Cup Series races for 7 wins and 79 top 10 finishes), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1989)
Johnny Berry, English soccer right winger (4 caps; Manchester United, Birmingham City), born in Aldershot, England (d. 1994)
Phillip Burton, American politician (US Representative from California, 1964-83), advocate for the disabled and the environment, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1983)
Bob Monkhouse, English comedian (Bonkers!) and game show host (The Golden Shot; Celebrity Squares), born in Beckenham, Kent, England (d. 2003)
Georgy Dobrovolsky, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 11), born in Odessa, Ukraine, Soviet Union (d. 1971)
Bill Schonely, American sports broadcaster (Portland Trail Blazers 1970-98), born in Norristown, Pennsylvania (d. 2023)
Yehudi Wyner, American pianist, Pulitzer Prize-winning composer (Chiavi in Mano), and educator, born in Calgary, Alberta, Canada
Edward Woodward, British actor (Breaker Morant; Wickerman; The Equalizer), and pop singer ("The Way You Look Tonight"), born in Croydon, Surrey (d. 2009)
Matt Poore, New Zealand cricket all-rounder (14 Tests 355 runs @ 15.43, 9 wickets), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 2020)
Hal Smith, American baseball catcher (MLB All-Star 1957, 59, 59²; St. Louis Cardinals, Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Barling, Arkansas (d. 2014)
Walter Horak, Austrian soccer striker (13 caps; Wiener Sport Club, Sochaux), born in Marchegg, Austria (d. 2019)
Christopher Lasch, American historian and author (The Culture of Narcissism), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1994)
Frank Cameron, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (19 Tests; 62 wickets; Otago CA), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 2023)
Philo Bregstein, Dutch writer and film director (The Past that Lives, In Search of Jewish Amsterdam), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands[1]
Alan Ameche, American College Football Hall of Fame fullback (Heisman Trophy 1954, U of Wisconsin; Pro Bowl 1955–1958, NFL C'ship 1958, 59; Baltimore Colts), born in Kenosha, Wisconsin (d. 1988)
Charles Wilson, American politician (Rep-D-TX, 1973-97), born in Trinity, Texas (d. 2010)

(Charles) "Pat" Boone, American singer ("Tutti Frutti"), actor (April Love; The Cross and The Switchblade; No More Mr. Nice Guy) and TV personality (The Pat Boone Chevy Showroom), born in Jacksonville, Florida
Peter Masterson, American actor (Ambush Bay), born in Houston, Texas (d. 2018)
Jack Kralick, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star 1964; no hitter 1962; Washington Senators/Minnesota Twins, Cleveland Indians), born in Youngstown, Ohio (d. 2012)
Jim George, American weightlifter (Olympic silver light heavyweight 1960; bronze 1956; WR snatch, clean & jerk 1956), born in Akron, Ohio
John Porter, American politician (Rep-R-IL, 1980-2001), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 2022)
Reverend Ike [Frederick J. Eikerenkoetter II], American televangelist (Joy of Living), born in Ridgeland, South Carolina (d. 2009)
Bekim Fehmiu, Yugoslavian actor (Black Sunday, Permission to Kill, Cagliostro), born in Sarajevo, Kingdom of Yugoslavia (d. 2010)
Gerald Scarfe, British illustrator (The Sunday Times; The New Yorker: Pink Floyd's "The Wall"), born in St. John's Wood, London, England
Colleen McCullough, Australian author (Tim, Indecent Obsession, The Thorn Birds), born in Wellington, NSW, Australia (d. 2015)
Rosaleen Linehan, Irish actress, born in Dublin, Ireland
Cleavon Little, American actor (Blazing Saddles; Toy Soldiers), born in Chickasha Oklahoma (d. 1992)
Kip Thorne, American Physicist (Nobel Prize 2017, LIGO), born in Logan, Utah
Rene Auberjonois, American actor and voice artist (Star Trek: Deep Space Nine, God of War), born in New York City (d. 2019)
Dean Chance, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1964, 67; Cy Young Award 1964; no-hitter 1967; LA / California Angels, Minnesota Twins), born in Wooster, Ohio (d. 2015)
